user_input: What is this project?
retrieved_contexts:
["Section: WHAT THE PROJECT IS
The problem it solves: recruiters reading a CV cannot tell verified fact from self-promotion, cannot quickly gauge how strong a candidate actually is in a given skill, and cannot interrogate a static document. This system turns a pile of documents into an interactive, grounded, honest representative that cites real evidence, refuses to answer what it does not know, and quantifies skill levels with a trained model instead of keyword guessing.

The whole system can run on either a 100% local open-source stack or an all-API cloud stack, selected purely through configuration with no code changes. The cloud configuration is what makes it deployable as an always-on, secured, low-cost service; the local configuration is what made it possible to build, train, and evaluate everything for free on the developer's own hardware.", 'Section: STRUCTURED STORE AND VECTOR STORE
The structured store is a small JSON file holding the verified profile fields plus the precomputed skill scores and their evidence. It is the source for get_structured_data and get_skill_proficiency, both of which therefore answer instantly and deterministically.

The vector store is ChromaDB, used as a persistent on-disk index with cosine similarity. Each candidate's chunks live in their own collection, with a paired "_summaries" collection for that candidate's summary index. This project overview lives in its own separate collection ("project_kb", plus its summary collection), which is what the search_project tool queries — keeping project knowledge cleanly isolated from candidate data so the two can never bleed into each other's retrieval.', "Section: DEPLOYMENT AND SECURITY
Several security properties are enforced by design. In production only the recruiter chat is served and the setup page is hidden, so recruiters can neither edit the profile nor trigger ingestion. The candidate's private data (the vector database and the structured profile) is kept out of any public repository. API keys live only in environment variables or managed secrets and are never committed. This very project-overview document is intentionally free of private information and secrets.

Running costs are minimal. The all-API serve path uses a fast, inexpensive Claude model for the agent and router, and a low-cost Voyage tier for embeddings and reranking — typically only a few dollars per month of light use — and the hosting itself can be free. The expensive work (training the scorer, generating synthetic data, parsing documents, running the full evaluation) all happens locally at build time and adds nothing to the hosting bill.", 'Section: CANDIDATE AI AGENT — COMPLETE PROJECT OVERVIEW
This document is the knowledge base for questions about the software project / system itself: what it is, how it is built, every major flow, and the engineering decisions behind it. It also describes how this AI agent itself works, so that when a recruiter asks the agent second-person questions ("how do you work?", "what reranker do you use?", "how were you built?", "what model are you?"), the agent can answer about its own architecture from this document. It contains no private candidate data, no personal information, and no API keys or secrets.', 'Section: CANDIDATE AI AGENT — COMPLETE PROJECT OVERVIEW
A note on "you" and "I": throughout this document, when the agent speaks about how "I" work or what "I" use, it is describing this very system. Questions about how the agent/assistant operates, is built, retrieves information, scores skills, was evaluated, or is deployed are all answered from this document. (In contrast, "your name", "your skills", "your experience" refer to the candidate's professional profile, which lives in the candidate's own documents and structured store, not here.)', 'Section: THE FOUR TOOLS
search_documents runs the full RAG pipeline over the candidate's unstructured documents for anything not covered by the other two tools: projects, detailed experience, what they did with a technology, achievements, certifications, and richer context. It is also the fallback when a skill was not among the assessed proficiencies.

search_project runs the same RAG pipeline, but over this project-overview document instead of the candidate's data, in a separate vector collection. It answers questions about the system itself and about how the agent works — architecture, technology stack, the RAG pipeline, the skill scorer, the evaluation suite, deployment, and design decisions — including second-person questions addressed to the agent ("how do you work?", "what model are you?", "what reranker do you use?"). This is the tool serving the very answer you are reading.', 'Section: WHAT THE PROJECT IS
The Candidate AI Agent is an AI-powered digital representative for a single job candidate. A recruiter opens a secured, always-available chat link and asks natural-language questions about the candidate; the agent answers accurately and professionally, grounded strictly in the candidate's own documents and a trained machine-learning scoring model. It is deliberately more than a standard "chat with your PDF" demo: it combines an agentic ReAct-style tool-calling architecture, an advanced multi-stage Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline, and a purpose-built, fine-tuned NLP model for skill-proficiency scoring, all wrapped in a rigorous multi-dimensional evaluation suite.', 'Section: THE EVALUATION DATA
Evaluation uses six synthetic candidates with deliberately varied document formats — PDF, DOCX, TXT, Markdown, and image/PNG specifically to exercise the OCR path. Each candidate has a golden dataset of around 75 questions spanning personal facts, education, experience, preferences, skills, proficiency levels, projects, certifications, complex multi-tool questions, and deliberately out-of-scope "negative" questions that the agent should refuse. A separate project-knowledge dataset asks questions about this system itself; those project questions are routed to search_project and evaluated the same way as the candidate document questions, so adding the new tool did not require a new scoring path. The pre-existing candidate evaluation data was left unchanged when the project dataset and tool were added.']
response:
This project is an AI-powered system designed to help recruiters evaluate job candidates more effectively by transforming their uploaded documents (e.g., CVs, certificates) into an interactive, evidence-based digital representative. Here's a summary:

### **Core Purpose**  
Recruiters struggle to discern verified facts from self-promotion in static documents. This system addresses this by:  
- Quantifying a candidate's skill proficiency using a trained machine learning model (1-5 scale).  
- Providing instant, deterministic answers to structured questions (e.g., education, experience) via a JSON-based structured store.  
- Allowing recruiters to ask natural language questions about a candidate’s projects, certifications, or technical expertise, with answers grounded in the candidate’s own documents.  

### **Key Features**  
1. **Dual Deployment Options**:  
   - **Local**: Open-source stack for development/training.  
   - **Cloud**: Low-cost, always-on API service with security measures (e.g., hidden setup pages, private vector databases).  

2. **Data Architecture**:  
   - **Structured Store**: JSON file holding verified profile fields and precomputed skill scores.  
   - **Vector Store**: ChromaDB for semantic search, isolating candidate data from project knowledge.  

3. **Security & Cost Efficiency**:  
   - Candidate data is never exposed in repositories or APIs.  
   - Uses inexpensive models (e.g., Claude, Voyage tier) for routing and embeddings, keeping hosting costs minimal.  

4. **AI Agent Capabilities**:  
   - Combines **ReAct-style tool-calling**, **multi-stage RAG pipeline**, and a **fine-tuned NLP model** for skill scoring.  
   - Answers questions about the candidate *and* the system itself (e.g., “How do you work?” via `search_project`).  

### **Evaluation**  
- Tested on synthetic candidates with varied document formats (PDF, DOCX, OCR-ed images).  
- Includes 75+ questions per candidate, covering skills, projects, and out-of-scope “negative” queries (which the system refuses to answer).  

This system bridges the gap between static resumes and dynamic, evidence-based candidate evaluation. Let me know if you’d like details about specific components!
reference: It is an AI-powered digital representative for a job candidate: a recruiter chats with it over a secured link and it answers questions about the candidate, grounded in the candidate's own documents and a trained scoring model. It combines an agentic ReAct-style architecture with an advanced RAG pipeline.

The system is an AI-powered recruitment tool that enables recruiters to interactively assess candidates by analyzing their uploaded documents (e.g., CVs, certificates). It combines **ReAct-style tool-calling architecture** with a **multi-stage RAG pipeline** to retrieve, contextualize, and generate evidence-based responses. Key features include:

1. **Skill Proficiency Scoring**: A fine-tuned NLP model evaluates technical skills (e.g., Python, AWS) on a 1-5 scale using machine learning, avoiding keyword-only assessments.  
2. **Flexible Architecture**: Operates via open-source local tools or cloud APIs, with separate "build" (document processing/model training) and "serve" (deployment) phases.  
3. **Deployment Options**: Optimized for low-cost, secure cloud operations or local development without code changes.  
4. **Evaluation Suite**: Uses multi-dimensional metrics to validate system performance.  

The system prioritizes transparency, providing quantifiable insights into a candidate’s expertise while maintaining flexibility for integration into existing workflows.
reference: An agentic recruiter-facing chat that routes between verified structured facts, a trained 1-5 skill-proficiency scorer, document RAG, and project RAG. It has a build phase (local ingestion, embedding, and skill scoring) and an always-on serve phase (retrieval plus LLM calls).

user_input: What reranker does the project use?
retrieved_contexts:
['Section: PLUGGABLE PROVIDERS
The reranker layer is either the Voyage rerank API (production default), a local Qwen3-Reranker, or "none" to skip reranking. The Qwen3 reranker is notable: it is a causal language model, not a classification cross-encoder, and is scored — per its model card — by reading the probability of the "yes" token at the final position of a query/document relevance prompt, rather than with a naive classification head.

The local backends are constructed lazily, so importing the embedder or reranker modules in the production (API) configuration never imports PyTorch or transformers. This is the mechanism that keeps the serve image torch-free while preserving the original "built entirely from local open-source models" story.', 'Section: THE FOUR TOOLS
search_documents runs the full RAG pipeline over the candidate's unstructured documents for anything not covered by the other two tools: projects, detailed experience, what they did with a technology, achievements, certifications, and richer context. user_input: How does the skill proficiency estimation work?
retrieved_contexts:
['Section: SKILL PROFICIENCY ESTIMATION — THE FLAGSHIP FEATURE
Rather than leaving "how good is the candidate at X?" to keyword matching, the project trains a dedicated NLP model to score each skill from 1 to 5 directly from the candidate's own documents. The scale is ordinal and defined as: 1 = awareness (a passing mention), 2 = working familiarity, 3 = competent / day-to-day use, 4 = strong / leads work in it, 5 = expert / authority.

At setup time, for each skill the candidate lists, the system retrieves that skill's supporting evidence chunks from the candidate's documents (routing and summary are off here, because a bare skill name is always a specific query) and runs the trained scorer. The result — a level plus the exact evidence chunks it was based on — is written into the structured store. The recruiter chat then surfaces this verified, model-grounded level instantly via get_skill_proficiency instead of recomputing or guessing at query time.', 'Section: THE FOUR TOOLS
get_structured_data returns fixed, guaranteed-accurate facts from the structured JSON store: full name, email, country code, phone, LinkedIn, GitHub, education (degrees, fields, institutions, GPAs), years of experience, current role, desired job title, job description, monthly salary expectation, preferred location, availability, work type, and openness to relocation. These are exact fields, never retrieved or paraphrased.

get_skill_proficiency returns the candidate's proficiency LEVEL (1 to 5) in a named skill, inferred by the trained scoring model from the candidate's documents, together with the supporting evidence chunks. With no skill named it lists every assessed skill ranked by level. This is the tool for any "how good / how strong / how proficient / how skilled / how experienced" question, or to rate or rank skills. If a requested skill was not assessed, the tool says so and the agent falls back to document search.', "Section: THE CORE DESIGN DECISION: BUILD VS SERVE
The single most important architectural decision is the split between a build phase and a serve phase.

The build phase runs locally and only occasionally. The candidate uploads documents; the system parses them, performs section-aware chunking, embeds the chunks into a vector store, generates per-document summaries, and runs the trained scoring model to estimate skill proficiency. This produces two persistent artifacts: a ChromaDB vector index (the unstructured knowledge) and a small JSON structured store of verified facts plus precomputed skill scores and their evidence. All heavyweight machine learning — document parsing, OCR, the DeBERTa skill scorer, and optionally a local embedder and reranker — only ever runs in this phase, on the candidate's own machine.", 'Section: SKILL PROFICIENCY ESTIMATION — THE FLAGSHIP FEATURE
The scorer itself is a fine-tuned DeBERTa-v3 transformer with a CORAL ordinal regression head. CORAL is used because the 1-to-5 levels are ordered, not arbitrary categories — confusing a 4 with a 5 is a smaller error than confusing a 1 with a 5, and an ordinal head encodes that structure. The model reads the skill name plus its evidence chunks, serialized in exactly the format it saw in training ("{skill} {sep} {chunk1} {chunk2} ..."). The best-performing configuration fine-tunes the top transformer layers; head-only and plain-softmax classifier variants were also trained and compared, and the ordinal CORAL configuration won. Because the scorer (and document parsing) only run at build time, neither is ever deployed to the always-on service.', 'Section: THE TWO USER FLOWS AND PAGES
The application is a multi-page Streamlit app with two pages.

The Candidate Setup page is for the candidate only. Here the candidate uploads supporting documents (CV, transcripts, certificates, recommendation letters) in PDF, DOCX, TXT, MD, PNG, or JPG form; fills in structured profile fields (contact details, education, experience, job preferences); lists the skills they want assessed; and triggers skill-proficiency estimation and ingestion. This page can edit the profile and run the heavy pipeline, so in production it is hidden entirely — a recruiter can never reach it, edit the profile, or trigger ingestion.', "Section: WHAT THE PROJECT IS
The problem it solves: recruiters reading a CV cannot tell verified fact from self-promotion, cannot quickly gauge how strong a candidate actually is in a given skill, and cannot interrogate a static document. This system turns a pile of documents into an interactive, grounded, honest representative that cites real evidence, refuses to answer what it does not know, and quantifies skill levels with a trained model instead of keyword guessing.

The whole system can run on either a 100% local open-source stack or an all-API cloud stack, selected purely through configuration with no code changes. The cloud configuration is what makes it deployable as an always-on, secured, low-cost service; the local configuration is what made it possible to build, train, and evaluate everything for free on the developer's own hardware.", 'Section: STRUCTURED STORE AND VECTOR STORE
The structured store is a small JSON file holding the verified profile fields plus the precomputed skill scores and their evidence. It is the source for get_structured_data and get_skill_proficiency, both of which therefore answer instantly and deterministically.

The vector store is ChromaDB, used as a persistent on-disk index with cosine similarity. Each candidate's chunks live in their own collection, with a paired "_summaries" collection for that candidate's summary index. This project overview lives in its own separate collection ("project_kb", plus its summary collection), which is what the search_project tool queries — keeping project knowledge cleanly isolated from candidate data so the two can never bleed into each other's retrieval.', 'Section: CANDIDATE AI AGENT — COMPLETE PROJECT OVERVIEW
A note on "you" and "I": throughout this document, when the agent speaks about how "I" work or what "I" use, it is describing this very system. Questions about how the agent/assistant operates, is built, retrieves information, scores skills, was evaluated, or is deployed are all answered from this document. (In contrast, "your name", "your skills", "your experience" refer to the candidate's professional profile, which lives in the candidate's own documents and structured store, not here.)']
response:
The skill proficiency estimation works through a dedicated NLP model trained to score skills on a 1–5 scale (1 = awareness, 5 = expert) directly from the candidate's documents. Here's the process:

1. **Build Phase**:  
   - The candidate uploads documents (CV, certificates, etc.), which are parsed, chunked, and embedded into a vector store (ChromaDB).  
   - For each skill listed, the system retrieves relevant evidence chunks and feeds them into the trained **DeBERTa-v3 model** with a **CORAL ordinal regression head**. This architecture handles the ordered nature of skill levels (e.g., confusion between 4 and 5 is less severe than between 1 and 5).  

2. **Scoring**:  
   - The model evaluates the evidence chunks for the skill, assigning a level (1–5) based on the depth of expertise (e.g., "expert" vs. "awareness").  
   - Results are stored in a structured JSON store, linking each skill to its level and supporting evidence.  

3. **Query-Time**:  
   - Recruiters use `get_skill_proficiency` to instantly retrieve precomputed, verified levels without reprocessing documents.  

This approach avoids keyword guessing, ensuring skill ratings are grounded in evidence from the candidate's own documents.
reference: A fine-tuned DeBERTa-v3 transformer with a CORAL ordinal regression head scores each listed skill from 1 to 5 using the evidence chunks retrieved from the candidate's documents. The level and its supporting evidence are saved into the structured store, and it runs only at setup time.

The project uses the following evaluation frameworks and tools:  
1. **RAGAS** (Retrieval Augmented Generation Assessment Suite) for measuring RAG quality, including metrics like faithfulness, context precision, recall, and answer relevancy.  
2. **DeepEval** with its **GEval** LLM-as-judge framework to assess answer correctness and appropriateness, including refusal accuracy.  
3. **Custom evaluation tools** for router accuracy, tool selection validation, and per-gate retrieval failure analysis (e.g., identifying whether recall or re-ranking missed critical chunks).  

These frameworks evaluate multiple dimensions, such as tool selection, RAG quality, and answer correctness, using synthetic datasets and real-world scenarios. The evaluation suite is designed to be rigorous and component-based, ensuring comprehensive validation of the system's performance.
reference: RAGAS for RAG quality (faithfulness, context precision and recall, answer relevancy) and DeepEval's GEval for answer correctness, plus custom evaluators for tool selection, broad/specific routing, refusal accuracy, and per-gate retrieval failure localization.

Router Accuracy: did the broad/specific router classify each query correctly against its labeled route?

Per-gate Retrieval Analysis: for specific document questions with known evidence, it localizes exactly where a relevant chunk was lost — at ingestion, at recall, or at re-ranking — using the pre-rerank fused pool the pipeline exposes.

Ingestion Quality: chunk statistics, coverage, and summary quality.

The harness seeds each candidate's data into a fresh collection, runs the pipeline over that candidate's questions, then scores every component on the combined result set and generates an HTML report (with companion JSON and CSV) showing both per-candidate and aggregate breakdowns. It supports checkpointing and resuming, so a long run can be interrupted and continued. Because the LLM judges can themselves run on a free local model, a full evaluation can cost nothing to run.", 'Section: THE RAG PIPELINE — RETRIEVAL
Retrieval is a multi-stage pipeline designed for both recall and precision.

First, query routing. An LLM router classifies each incoming query as BROAD (a general "who is this person / give me an overview" question) or SPECIFIC (any particular fact, skill, project, or detail). Broad queries are answered from the compact summary index, which gives a clean high-level picture; specific queries go through the full fusion path below.

Second, query expansion. For specific queries the LLM generates several query variations — a deliberate mix of short keyword-style queries, which favor sparse search, and natural-language queries, which favor dense vector search — to maximize recall across both retrieval methods.', 'Section: THE RAG PIPELINE — INGESTION

response: Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) is used in the retrieval pipeline to combine results from **dense vector search** (semantic similarity) and **sparse keyword search** (BM25 Okapi) into a unified ranked list. It scores each retrieval list independently by reciprocal rank, rewarding top results from any single list regardless of its length or origin. This fusion integrates keyword and semantic signals without requiring calibration between their raw scores, enhancing the system's ability to retrieve relevant information.
reference: RRF merges the ranked result lists from dense vector search and BM25 keyword search (across the expanded query variations) into a single fused ranking, scoring each list independently so the top of any list gets equal weight.

Documents are parsed with the Unstructured library, backed by PyMuPDF for PDFs, python-docx for Word files, and OCR via Tesseract and Pillow for images. The parser performs section-aware extraction rather than dumping flat text. It detects section headers — including unusual spaced-letter headers like "T E C H N I C A L  S K I L L S" via a dedicated regular expression — and merges consecutive elements under the same heading into one section block. It is careful not to mistake content for headers: "Title" elements that contain numbers, emails, or telephone markers (for example "GPA: 94.3" or a contact line) are kept as content, not promoted to section names.', 'Section: THE RAG PIPELINE — INGESTION
Alongside the per-chunk index, a separate summary index is built. For each document the LLM writes a short factual summary, which is embedded and stored in its own ChromaDB collection. This summary index powers the "broad" retrieval path described below. There is also a lighter in-memory ingestion path for text that is already plain (no file parsing, no summary) — used, for example, when the skill-scorer training corpus is ingested verbatim so the model sees exact text.', 'Section: THE TWO USER FLOWS AND PAGES
The application is a multi-page Streamlit app with two pages.

The Candidate Setup page is for the candidate only. Here the candidate uploads supporting documents (CV, transcripts, certificates, recommendation letters) in PDF, DOCX, TXT, MD, PNG, or JPG form; fills in structured profile fields (contact details, education, experience, job preferences); lists the skills they want assessed; and triggers skill-proficiency estimation and ingestion. This page can edit the profile and run the heavy pipeline, so in production it is hidden entirely — a recruiter can never reach it, edit the profile, or trigger ingestion.', "Section: THE CORE DESIGN DECISION: BUILD VS SERVE
The single most important architectural decision is the split between a build phase and a serve phase.

The build phase runs locally and only occasionally. The candidate uploads documents; the system parses them, performs section-aware chunking, embeds the chunks into a vector store, generates per-document summaries, and runs the trained scoring model to estimate skill proficiency. This produces two persistent artifacts: a ChromaDB vector index (the unstructured knowledge) and a small JSON structured store of verified facts plus precomputed skill scores and their evidence. All heavyweight machine learning — document parsing, OCR, the DeBERTa skill scorer, and optionally a local embedder and reranker — only ever runs in this phase, on the candidate's own machine.", 'Section: THE RAG PIPELINE — INGESTION
Each section is then split with a recursive character text splitter (about 1000 characters per chunk with 150 characters of overlap, preferring to break on paragraph, line, sentence, then word boundaries). Every chunk is prefixed with its section name for retrieval context ("Section: <name>") and stored with provenance metadata: the source file and a document id. This provenance is essential — it lets any retrieved chunk be traced back to its origin document, which is what makes retrieval evaluation against ground-truth evidence possible.', 'Section: THE RAG PIPELINE — RETRIEVAL
A deliberate design touch: the pipeline exposes the fused candidate pool from before re-ranking. The embeddings are asymmetric: documents and queries are embedded with different prefixes ("search_document:" versus "search_query:"), which matches how the embedding models were trained and improves retrieval quality. Because the stored document vectors bake in the document prefix, switching the embedding provider requires rebuilding the vector index.', 'Section: TECHNOLOGY STACK

Skill scoring: DeBERTa-v3 with a CORAL ordinal head, in PyTorch and Hugging Face Transformers. Ingestion: Unstructured, PyMuPDF, python-docx, Tesseract OCR, and section-aware recursive chunking via LangChain text splitters. Router Accuracy: did the broad/specific router classify each query correctly against its labeled route?

Per-gate Retrieval Analysis: for specific document questions with known evidence, it localizes exactly where a relevant chunk was lost — at ingestion, at recall, or at re-ranking — using the pre-rerank fused pool the pipeline exposes.

Ingestion Quality: chunk statistics, coverage, and summary quality.

The harness seeds each candidate's data into a fresh collection, runs the pipeline over that candidate's questions, then scores every component on the combined result set and generates an HTML report (with companion JSON and CSV) showing both per-candidate and aggregate breakdowns. Documents are ingested through a structured pipeline with the following key steps:

1. **Parsing & Extraction**  
   - Files (PDF, DOCX, TXT, etc.) are parsed using **Unstructured** with backend tools:

2. **Chunking & Storage**

4. **Build Phase**

user_input: What tools does the agent have?
retrieved_contexts:
["Section: AGENTIC TOOL CALLING — THE ReAct LOOP
The system does not blindly query a vector database for every question. Instead an LLM agent reasons about each question and chooses among four tools, and it can chain multiple tools within a single turn, including intelligent fallbacks.

The agent loop works as follows. The recruiter's message is appended to the conversation history. A per-turn working copy of the messages is sent to the LLM together with the system prompt and the four tool schemas. If the model responds with no tool calls, that response is the final answer. If the model requests one or more tools, the loop records the assistant's tool-call turn, executes every requested tool, feeds each tool result back as a tool message, and loops again — so the model can now either call another tool (for example, fall back to document search) or produce a final answer. A safety cap (five rounds) prevents infinite tool loops; if it is hit, the loop does one final pass with tools disabled, forcing the model to produce text.", 'Section: WHAT THE PROJECT IS
The Candidate AI Agent is an AI-powered digital representative for a single job candidate. A recruiter opens a secured, always-available chat link and asks natural-language questions about the candidate; the agent answers accurately and professionally, grounded strictly in the candidate's own documents and a trained machine-learning scoring model. It is deliberately more than a standard "chat with your PDF" demo: it combines an agentic ReAct-style tool-calling architecture, an advanced multi-stage Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline, and a purpose-built, fine-tuned NLP model for skill-proficiency scoring, all wrapped in a rigorous multi-dimensional evaluation suite.', "Section: THE FOUR TOOLS
The system prompt instructs the agent to distinguish the two RAG tools by subject: a question about the candidate goes to search_documents; a question about the software project or how the system/agent works goes to search_project. It also tells the agent never to guess, to keep answers concise and professional, to refuse out-of-scope questions, and to answer in the recruiter's language.", "Section: THE EVALUATION SUITE
The project ships a rigorous, component-based evaluation harness rather than ad-hoc spot checks. It runs the full agent over golden datasets and scores several independent dimensions.

Tool Selection: did the agent pick the correct tool — structured facts versus skill scorer versus candidate document search versus project search — for each question?

RAG Quality: RAGAS metrics (faithfulness, context precision, context recall, and answer relevancy) computed on the RAG-routed questions, using the retrieved contexts captured during the run.

Answer Correctness: DeepEval's GEval LLM-as-judge on every question, scoring whether the final answer is correct and appropriate, including whether refusals are worded correctly.

Refusal Accuracy: a confusion matrix checking that the agent refuses out-of-scope or inappropriate questions and does not refuse legitimate ones.

Router Accuracy: did the broad/specific router classify each query correctly against its labeled route?", 'Section: THE FOUR TOOLS
search_documents runs the full RAG pipeline over the candidate's unstructured documents for anything not covered by the other two tools: projects, detailed experience, what they did with a technology, achievements, certifications, and richer context. It is also the fallback when a skill was not among the assessed proficiencies.

search_project runs the same RAG pipeline, but over this project-overview document instead of the candidate's data, in a separate vector collection. It answers questions about the system itself and about how the agent works — architecture, technology stack, the RAG pipeline, the skill scorer, the evaluation suite, deployment, and design decisions — including second-person questions addressed to the agent ("how do you work?", "what model are you?", "what reranker do you use?"). This is the tool serving the very answer you are reading.', 'Section: NEUTRAL LLM CLIENT
Every LLM interaction in the project — the agent loop, the broad/specific query router, query expansion, and the build-time document summaries — goes through a single provider-neutral client. The client exposes two methods: complete() for simple prompt-to-text calls, and chat() for tool-calling conversations. It returns a normalized Reply object carrying the assistant text and any tool calls in a provider-independent shape.', "Section: DEPLOYMENT AND SECURITY
Several security properties are enforced by design. In production only the recruiter chat is served and the setup page is hidden, so recruiters can neither edit the profile nor trigger ingestion. The candidate's private data (the vector database and the structured profile) is kept out of any public repository. API keys live only in environment variables or managed secrets and are never committed. This very project-overview document is intentionally free of private information and secrets.

Running costs are minimal. The all-API serve path uses a fast, inexpensive Claude model for the agent and router, and a low-cost Voyage tier for embeddings and reranking — typically only a few dollars per month of light use — and the hosting itself can be free. The expensive work (training the scorer, generating synthetic data, parsing documents, running the full evaluation) all happens locally at build time and adds nothing to the hosting bill.", 'Section: DESIGN PRINCIPLES AND WHAT MAKES IT NOTABLE
The standout engineering choices are: a trained, evidence-grounded skill scorer instead of keyword guessing; an agent that routes between verified facts, the scorer, candidate document search, and project search with intelligent fallbacks; a hybrid, fused, re-ranked retrieval pipeline fronted by a broad/specific router and query expansion; a serious multi-dimensional evaluation suite, including the per-gate retrieval failure localization that pinpoints whether recall or re-ranking lost a chunk; a fully synthetic, validated training-data pipeline built because the needed labels did not exist; and a clean build-versus-serve split with three pluggable provider layers that lets one codebase run entirely on free local open-source models or deploy as a cheap, secured, always-on cloud service']
response:
The agent has four core tools, each serving a specific purpose in retrieving and processing information:

1. **`get_structured_data`**  
   Retrieves fixed, verified fields from the candidate's profile (e.g., contact info, education, job preferences).

2. **`get_skill_proficiency`**  
   Rates the candidate's proficiency (1-5) in specific skills (e.g., Python, AWS) using a trained scoring model, with evidence from their documents.

3. **`search_documents`**  
   Semantically searches the candidate's uploaded documents (CV, projects, certifications) for detailed experience, projects, or context not covered by structured data or skill proficiencies.

4. **`search_project`**  
   Answers questions about the system itself or how the agent works (e.g., RAG pipeline, skill-scoring model, architecture) or second-person queries about the agent's operations.

These tools work together in a ReAct loop, with intelligent fallbacks, to ensure accurate, context-rich responses. Let me know if you'd like details about a specific tool!
reference: Four tools: get_structured_data for verified facts, get_skill_proficiency for the 1-5 trained skill score, search_documents for RAG over the candidate's documents, and search_project for RAG over the project's own documentation.

response:
The query router is a key component of the RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) pipeline. Its primary function is to **determine which tool to call** based on the user's question. For example:
- Routes questions about structured data (e.g., "What is their email?") to `get_structured_data`.
- Directs skill proficiency queries (e.g., "How good are they at Python?") to `get_skill_proficiency`.
- Sends open-ended or contextual questions (e.g., "Describe a project they worked on") to `search_documents`.

This ensures the right tool is used for the right task, enabling precise, evidence-based answers by leveraging the candidate’s documents and structured data.
reference: An LLM router classifies each query as BROAD (a general overview question) or SPECIFIC; broad queries are answered from the summary index, while specific queries go through the full query-expansion, hybrid-fusion, and re-ranking path.

response:
The **build-vs-serve split** is a core architectural design decision that separates two distinct phases of the system's operation:

### **Build Phase**  
- **Purpose**: Handles all preprocessing and heavy computation locally.  
- **Tasks**:  
  - Parses candidate documents (CVs, certificates, etc.).  
  - Performs section-aware chunking, embedding, and summarization.  
  - Runs the trained **skill proficiency model** to score skills (1-5) with evidence.  
  - Generates two artifacts:  
    - A **ChromaDB vector index** (unstructured knowledge).  
    - A **structured JSON store** of verified facts and precomputed skill scores.  
- **Dependencies**: Includes machine learning models (e.g., DeBERTa for scoring, OCR, embedders) and requires local computation.  

### **Serve Phase**  
- **Purpose**: Hosted, always-on chat interface for recruiters.  
- **Tasks**:  
  - Reads prebuilt artifacts (no heavy computation).  
  - Performs retrieval (vector + hybrid BM25) and LLM-powered responses.  
  - Avoids deploying heavy models (no PyTorch, transformers, or Unstructured libraries).  
- **Deployment**: Lightweight, uses inexpensive models (e.g., Claude for agents, Voyage for reranking) and minimal dependencies (listed in `requirements.txt`).  

### **Key Benefits**  
- **Cost Efficiency**: Heavy ML work happens during build (local, no hosting costs), while serve is cheap and scalable.  
- **Security**: Private data (vector DB, structured profiles) is never exposed in repositories or APIs.  
- **Portability**: The split allows the same codebase to run locally (for development/proofing) or as a cloud service.  

This separation enables a system that is both sophisticated (via advanced ML) and simple to host (lean serve dependencies).
reference: The build phase runs locally and parses documents, embeds them, and runs the skill scorer to produce the vector index and structured store. The serve phase is hosted and always on and only performs retrieval and LLM calls, avoiding any heavy local model so it fits cheap hosting.

['Section: SKILL PROFICIENCY ESTIMATION — THE FLAGSHIP FEATURE
Rather than leaving "how good is the candidate at X?" to keyword matching, the project trains a dedicated NLP model to score each skill from 1 to 5 directly from the candidate's own documents. The scale is ordinal and defined as: 1 = awareness (a passing mention), 2 = working familiarity, 3 = competent / day-to-day use, 4 = strong / leads work in it, 5 = expert / authority.

At setup time, for each skill the candidate lists, the system retrieves that skill's supporting evidence chunks from the candidate's documents (routing and summary are off here, because a bare skill name is always a specific query) and runs the trained scorer. The result — a level plus the exact evidence chunks it was based on — is written into the structured store. The recruiter chat then surfaces this verified, model-grounded level instantly via get_skill_proficiency instead of recomputing or guessing at query time.', 'Section: THE FOUR TOOLS
get_structured_data returns fixed, guaranteed-accurate facts from the structured JSON store: full name, email, country code, phone, LinkedIn, GitHub, education (degrees, fields, institutions, GPAs), years of experience, current role, desired job title, job description, monthly salary expectation, preferred location, availability, work type, and openness to relocation. These are exact fields, never retrieved or paraphrased.

get_skill_proficiency returns the candidate's proficiency LEVEL (1 to 5) in a named skill, inferred by the trained scoring model from the candidate's documents, together with the supporting evidence chunks. With no skill named it lists every assessed skill ranked by level. This is the tool for any "how good / how strong / how proficient / how skilled / how experienced" question, or to rate or rank skills. If a requested skill was not assessed, the tool says so and the agent falls back to document search.', 'Section: SKILL PROFICIENCY ESTIMATION — THE FLAGSHIP FEATURE
The scorer itself is a fine-tuned DeBERTa-v3 transformer with a CORAL ordinal regression head. CORAL is used because the 1-to-5 levels are ordered, not arbitrary categories — confusing a 4 with a 5 is a smaller error than confusing a 1 with a 5, and an ordinal head encodes that structure. The model reads the skill name plus its evidence chunks, serialized in exactly the format it saw in training ("{skill} {sep} {chunk1} {chunk2} ..."). The best-performing configuration fine-tunes the top transformer layers; head-only and plain-softmax classifier variants were also trained and compared, and the ordinal CORAL configuration won. Because the scorer (and document parsing) only run at build time, neither is ever deployed to the always-on service.', 'Section: CANDIDATE AI AGENT — COMPLETE PROJECT OVERVIEW
A note on "you" and "I": throughout this document, when the agent speaks about how "I" work or what "I" use, it is describing this very system. Questions about how the agent/assistant operates, is built, retrieves information, scores skills, was evaluated, or is deployed are all answered from this document. (In contrast, "your name", "your skills", "your experience" refer to the candidate's professional profile, which lives in the candidate's own documents and structured store, not here.)', 'Section: SYNTHETIC TRAINING DATA GENERATION
Training the skill scorer required labeled (skill, evidence, level) data that does not exist publicly, so the project includes a full synthetic data generation system. It builds realistic candidate personas and archetypes, generates documents containing controlled skill evidence at known proficiency levels, tracks phrase usage across generations to avoid repetition, sanitizes the output, and validates the corpus with statistical checks, cross-validation, and shortcut-detection analysis — the latter to ensure the model learns from genuine evidence rather than spurious surface cues (such as a level always co-occurring with a particular phrase). Generation uses asynchronous LLM calls for throughput. The result is a labeled dataset large and clean enough to fine-tune an ordinal scorer that generalizes to real documents.', 'Section: THE EVALUATION DATA
Evaluation uses six synthetic candidates with deliberately varied document formats — PDF, DOCX, TXT, Markdown, and image/PNG specifically to exercise the OCR path. Each candidate has a golden dataset of around 75 questions spanning personal facts, education, experience, preferences, skills, proficiency levels, projects, certifications, complex multi-tool questions, and deliberately out-of-scope "negative" questions that the agent should refuse. A separate project-knowledge dataset asks questions about this system itself; those project questions are routed to search_project and evaluated the same way as the candidate document questions, so adding the new tool did not require a new scoring path. The pre-existing candidate evaluation data was left unchanged when the project dataset and tool were added.', 'Section: DESIGN PRINCIPLES AND WHAT MAKES IT NOTABLE
. The guiding principles throughout are grounding (never answer beyond the evidence), verifiability (every skill level and fact traces back to a source), honesty (refuse the unknown and the out-of-scope), and portability (local for building and proving, cloud for serving).', "Section: THE CORE DESIGN DECISION: BUILD VS SERVE
The single most important architectural decision is the split between a build phase and a serve phase.

The build phase runs locally and only occasionally. The candidate uploads documents; the system parses them, performs section-aware chunking, embeds the chunks into a vector store, generates per-document summaries, and runs the trained scoring model to estimate skill proficiency. This produces two persistent artifacts: a ChromaDB vector index (the unstructured knowledge) and a small JSON structured store of verified facts plus precomputed skill scores and their evidence. All heavyweight machine learning — document parsing, OCR, the DeBERTa skill scorer, and optionally a local embedder and reranker — only ever runs in this phase, on the candidate's own machine."]
response:
The 1 to 5 skill proficiency levels are ordinal, meaning they represent ordered degrees of mastery rather than arbitrary categories. Here's what each level signifies:

1. **1 (Awareness)**: A passing mention or superficial reference to the skill.  
2. **2 (Working familiarity)**: Basic understanding and limited practical use.  
3. **3 (Competent)**: Proficient in day-to-day tasks and standard applications.  
4. **4 (Strong)**: Demonstrates leadership or advanced capabilities in the skill.  
5. **5 (Expert)**: Authority-level expertise, often with contributions to the field or mentorship.  

These levels are determined by a trained NLP model analyzing the candidate’s documents. The model identifies evidence (e.g., project details, certifications, or code samples) and assigns a level based on the depth and context of that evidence. The scoring uses a fine-tuned DeBERTa-v3 model with a CORAL ordinal regression head to ensure the ordered nature of the scale is preserved.
reference: 1 is awareness (a passing mention), 2 is working familiarity, 3 is competent or day-to-day use, 4 is strong or leads work in it, and 5 is expert or authority.

user_input: How was the training data for the skill scorer created?
retrieved_contexts:
['Section: SYNTHETIC TRAINING DATA GENERATION
Training the skill scorer required labeled (skill, evidence, level) data that does not exist publicly, so the project includes a full synthetic data generation system. It builds realistic candidate personas and archetypes, generates documents containing controlled skill evidence at known proficiency levels, tracks phrase usage across generations to avoid repetition, sanitizes the output, and validates the corpus with statistical checks, cross-validation, and shortcut-detection analysis — the latter to ensure the model learns from genuine evidence rather than spurious surface cues (such as a level always co-occurring with a particular phrase). Generation uses asynchronous LLM calls for throughput. The result is a labeled dataset large and clean enough to fine-tune an ordinal scorer that generalizes to real documents.', 'Section: DESIGN PRINCIPLES AND WHAT MAKES IT NOTABLE
The standout engineering choices are: a trained, evidence-grounded skill scorer instead of keyword guessing; an agent that routes between verified facts, the scorer, candidate document search, and project search with intelligent fallbacks; a hybrid, fused, re-ranked retrieval pipeline fronted by a broad/specific router and query expansion; a serious multi-dimensional evaluation suite, including the per-gate retrieval failure localization that pinpoints whether recall or re-ranking lost a chunk; a fully synthetic, validated training-data pipeline built because the needed labels did not exist; and a clean build-versus-serve split with three pluggable provider layers that lets one codebase run entirely on free local open-source models or deploy as a cheap, secured, always-on cloud service', 'Section: SKILL PROFICIENCY ESTIMATION — THE FLAGSHIP FEATURE
Rather than leaving "how good is the candidate at X?" to keyword matching, the project trains a dedicated NLP model to score each skill from 1 to 5 directly from the candidate's own documents. The scale is ordinal and defined as: 1 = awareness (a passing mention), 2 = working familiarity, 3 = competent / day-to-day use, 4 = strong / leads work in it, 5 = expert / authority.

At setup time, for each skill the candidate lists, the system retrieves that skill's supporting evidence chunks from the candidate's documents (routing and summary are off here, because a bare skill name is always a specific query) and runs the trained scorer. The result — a level plus the exact evidence chunks it was based on — is written into the structured store. The recruiter chat then surfaces this verified, model-grounded level instantly via get_skill_proficiency instead of recomputing or guessing at query time.', 'Section: SKILL PROFICIENCY ESTIMATION — THE FLAGSHIP FEATURE
The scorer itself is a fine-tuned DeBERTa-v3 transformer with a CORAL ordinal regression head. CORAL is used because the 1-to-5 levels are ordered, not arbitrary categories — confusing a 4 with a 5 is a smaller error than confusing a 1 with a 5, and an ordinal head encodes that structure. The model reads the skill name plus its evidence chunks, serialized in exactly the format it saw in training ("{skill} {sep} {chunk1} {chunk2} ..."). The best-performing configuration fine-tunes the top transformer layers; head-only and plain-softmax classifier variants were also trained and compared, and the ordinal CORAL configuration won. Because the scorer (and document parsing) only run at build time, neither is ever deployed to the always-on service.', 'Section: THE RAG PIPELINE — INGESTION
Alongside the per-chunk index, a separate summary index is built. For each document the LLM writes a short factual summary, which is embedded and stored in its own ChromaDB collection. This summary index powers the "broad" retrieval path described below. There is also a lighter in-memory ingestion path for text that is already plain (no file parsing, no summary) — used, for example, when the skill-scorer training corpus is ingested verbatim so the model sees exact text.', "Section: THE CORE DESIGN DECISION: BUILD VS SERVE
The single most important architectural decision is the split between a build phase and a serve phase.

The build phase runs locally and only occasionally. The candidate uploads documents; the system parses them, performs section-aware chunking, embeds the chunks into a vector store, generates per-document summaries, and runs the trained scoring model to estimate skill proficiency. This produces two persistent artifacts: a ChromaDB vector index (the unstructured knowledge) and a small JSON structured store of verified facts plus precomputed skill scores and their evidence. All heavyweight machine learning — document parsing, OCR, the DeBERTa skill scorer, and optionally a local embedder and reranker — only ever runs in this phase, on the candidate's own machine.", "Section: DEPLOYMENT AND SECURITY
Several security properties are enforced by design. In production only the recruiter chat is served and the setup page is hidden, so recruiters can neither edit the profile nor trigger ingestion. The candidate's private data (the vector database and the structured profile) is kept out of any public repository. API keys live only in environment variables or managed secrets and are never committed. This very project-overview document is intentionally free of private information and secrets.

Running costs are minimal. The all-API serve path uses a fast, inexpensive Claude model for the agent and router, and a low-cost Voyage tier for embeddings and reranking — typically only a few dollars per month of light use — and the hosting itself can be free. The expensive work (training the scorer, generating synthetic data, parsing documents, running the full evaluation) all happens locally at build time and adds nothing to the hosting bill.", 'Section: THE FOUR TOOLS
search_documents runs the full RAG pipeline over the candidate's unstructured documents for anything not covered by the other two tools: projects, detailed experience, what they did with a technology, achievements, certifications, and richer context. It is also the fallback when a skill was not among the assessed proficiencies.

search_project runs the same RAG pipeline, but over this project-overview document instead of the candidate's data, in a separate vector collection. It answers questions about the system itself and about how the agent works — architecture, technology stack, the RAG pipeline, the skill scorer, the evaluation suite, deployment, and design decisions — including second-person questions addressed to the agent ("how do you work?", "what model are you?", "what reranker do you use?"). This is the tool serving the very answer you are reading.']
response:
The training data for the skill scorer was synthetically generated because no public labeled datasets exist for this purpose. The system builds realistic candidate personas and generates documents with controlled skill evidence at known proficiency levels. Key steps include:  
1. **Synthetic Data Generation**: Creating diverse candidate "archetypes" and documents containing deliberate skill evidence (e.g., "Python" proficiency levels).  
2. **Validation**: Statistical checks, cross-validation, and shortcut-detection analysis to ensure the model learns from genuine evidence, not surface patterns.  
3. **Model Training**: A fine-tuned DeBERTa-v3 transformer with a CORAL ordinal regression head (for 1-5 skill levels) was trained on this data.  
4. **Build vs. Serve Split**: All heavy processing (scoring, document parsing) occurs during the local "build" phase, while the "serve" phase handles lightweight queries.  

This approach ensures the skill scorer generalizes to real candidate documents without relying on proprietary labels.
reference: It was synthetically generated: realistic candidate personas and archetypes with controlled skill evidence at known proficiency levels, phrase tracking to avoid repetition, sanitization, and validation via statistical checks, cross-validation, and shortcut detection.

user_input: Which LLM does the project use?
retrieved_contexts:
['Section: PLUGGABLE PROVIDERS
Three layers are pluggable purely through configuration, with no code changes, and this is what lets the same codebase run fully local or fully cloud.

The LLM layer is either Claude via the Anthropic API (production default) or a local Ollama model. All LLM uses — agent, router, query expansion, build summaries — go through the one provider-neutral client described above, with prompt caching on the Anthropic path.

The embeddings layer is either the Voyage AI API (production default, no local model) or a local nomic-embed-text model via sentence-transformers. Both are asymmetric document/query embedders.', 'Section: NEUTRAL LLM CLIENT
Every LLM interaction in the project — the agent loop, the broad/specific query router, query expansion, and the build-time document summaries — goes through a single provider-neutral client. The client exposes two methods: complete() for simple prompt-to-text calls, and chat() for tool-calling conversations. It returns a normalized Reply object carrying the assistant text and any tool calls in a provider-independent shape.', 'Section: SYNTHETIC TRAINING DATA GENERATION
Training the skill scorer required labeled (skill, evidence, level) data that does not exist publicly, so the project includes a full synthetic data generation system. It builds realistic candidate personas and archetypes, generates documents containing controlled skill evidence at known proficiency levels, tracks phrase usage across generations to avoid repetition, sanitizes the output, and validates the corpus with statistical checks, cross-validation, and shortcut-detection analysis — the latter to ensure the model learns from genuine evidence rather than spurious surface cues (such as a level always co-occurring with a particular phrase). Generation uses asynchronous LLM calls for throughput. The result is a labeled dataset large and clean enough to fine-tune an ordinal scorer that generalizes to real documents.', "Section: THE EVALUATION SUITE
The project ships a rigorous, component-based evaluation harness rather than ad-hoc spot checks. It runs the full agent over golden datasets and scores several independent dimensions.

Tool Selection: did the agent pick the correct tool — structured facts versus skill scorer versus candidate document search versus project search — for each question?

RAG Quality: RAGAS metrics (faithfulness, context precision, context recall, and answer relevancy) computed on the RAG-routed questions, using the retrieved contexts captured during the run.

Answer Correctness: DeepEval's GEval LLM-as-judge on every question, scoring whether the final answer is correct and appropriate, including whether refusals are worded correctly.

Refusal Accuracy: a confusion matrix checking that the agent refuses out-of-scope or inappropriate questions and does not refuse legitimate ones.

Router Accuracy: did the broad/specific router classify each query correctly against its labeled route?", 'Section: TECHNOLOGY STACK
Frontend and app framework: Streamlit, multi-page. LLM and orchestration: Anthropic Claude API (or local Ollama), ReAct-style tool calling, prompt caching, a provider-neutral client. Embeddings: Voyage AI (or local nomic-embed via sentence-transformers). Retrieval: ChromaDB for dense vectors, BM25 Okapi for sparse search, Reciprocal Rank Fusion, and a cross-encoder reranker (Voyage rerank or Qwen3-Reranker). Skill scoring: DeBERTa-v3 with a CORAL ordinal head, in PyTorch and Hugging Face Transformers. Ingestion: Unstructured, PyMuPDF, python-docx, Tesseract OCR, and section-aware recursive chunking via LangChain text splitters. Evaluation: RAGAS and DeepEval (GEval), plus custom tool, router, refusal, and per-gate retrieval evaluators, with HTML, JSON, and CSV reports. Configuration: a central settings module resolving environment variables, then Streamlit secrets, then defaults.', 'Section: THE CORE DESIGN DECISION: BUILD VS SERVE
The serve phase is the hosted, always-on recruiter chat. It only reads the prebuilt artifacts and performs retrieval plus LLM calls. It deliberately contains no heavy local model, so the serve image is small (no PyTorch, no transformers, no Unstructured) and fits free or near-free hosting tiers. This separation is what makes the project simultaneously sophisticated to build and cheap and simple to host. The lean serve dependencies are listed in requirements.txt; the full build/eval/training stack is in requirements-dev.txt.', 'Section: GPU MEMORY MANAGEMENT DURING SCORING
When the local stack is used, the DeBERTa scorer and the local Ollama LLM contend for the same GPU. The DeBERTa model with disentangled attention is memory-heavy, and a resident Ollama model with multiple parallel slots can occupy most of the card. To run both on a modest GPU, the build phase time-slices the device: just before the scoring burst it asks the Ollama server to evict its model from VRAM, runs the scorer, and then unloads the scorer and clears the CUDA cache so Ollama can reload (honoring its parallelism) for the question-answering work. The batch size of the scorer is also tunable via an environment variable. None of this applies to the API stack, where no local model is resident.', "Section: AGENTIC TOOL CALLING — THE ReAct LOOP
The system does not blindly query a vector database for every question. Instead an LLM agent reasons about each question and chooses among four tools, and it can chain multiple tools within a single turn, including intelligent fallbacks.

The agent loop works as follows. The recruiter's message is appended to the conversation history. A per-turn working copy of the messages is sent to the LLM together with the system prompt and the four tool schemas. If the model responds with no tool calls, that response is the final answer. If the model requests one or more tools, the loop records the assistant's tool-call turn, executes every requested tool, feeds each tool result back as a tool message, and loops again — so the model can now either call another tool (for example, fall back to document search) or produce a final answer. A safety cap (five rounds) prevents infinite tool loops; if it is hit, the loop does one final pass with tools disabled, forcing the model to produce text."]
response: The project uses either **Claude via the Anthropic API** (production default) or a **local Ollama model** for its LLM layer. This is managed through a provider-neutral client that abstracts the underlying implementation, allowing seamless switching between these options.
reference: Claude via the Anthropic API by default, or a local Ollama model; the choice is configurable, and the same provider-neutral client serves the agent loop, the router, query expansion, and build-time summaries.

user_input: What is the per-gate retrieval analysis?
retrieved_contexts:
["Section: THE EVALUATION SUITE
Router Accuracy: did the broad/specific router classify each query correctly against its labeled route?

Per-gate Retrieval Analysis: for specific document questions with known evidence, it localizes exactly where a relevant chunk was lost — at ingestion, at recall, or at re-ranking — using the pre-rerank fused pool the pipeline exposes.

Ingestion Quality: chunk statistics, coverage, and summary quality.

The harness seeds each candidate's data into a fresh collection, runs the pipeline over that candidate's questions, then scores every component on the combined result set and generates an HTML report (with companion JSON and CSV) showing both per-candidate and aggregate breakdowns. It supports checkpointing and resuming, so a long run can be interrupted and continued. Because the LLM judges can themselves run on a free local model, a full evaluation can cost nothing to run.", 'Section: DESIGN PRINCIPLES AND WHAT MAKES IT NOTABLE
The standout engineering choices are: a trained, evidence-grounded skill scorer instead of keyword guessing; an agent that routes between verified facts, the scorer, candidate document search, and project search with intelligent fallbacks; a hybrid, fused, re-ranked retrieval pipeline fronted by a broad/specific router and query expansion; a serious multi-dimensional evaluation suite, including the per-gate retrieval failure localization that pinpoints whether recall or re-ranking lost a chunk; a fully synthetic, validated training-data pipeline built because the needed labels did not exist; and a clean build-versus-serve split with three pluggable provider layers that lets one codebase run entirely on free local open-source models or deploy as a cheap, secured, always-on cloud service', 'Section: TECHNOLOGY STACK
Frontend and app framework: Streamlit, multi-page. LLM and orchestration: Anthropic Claude API (or local Ollama), ReAct-style tool calling, prompt caching, a provider-neutral client. Embeddings: Voyage AI (or local nomic-embed via sentence-transformers). Retrieval: ChromaDB for dense vectors, BM25 Okapi for sparse search, Reciprocal Rank Fusion, and a cross-encoder reranker (Voyage rerank or Qwen3-Reranker). Skill scoring: DeBERTa-v3 with a CORAL ordinal head, in PyTorch and Hugging Face Transformers. Ingestion: Unstructured, PyMuPDF, python-docx, Tesseract OCR, and section-aware recursive chunking via LangChain text splitters. Evaluation: RAGAS and DeepEval (GEval), plus custom tool, router, refusal, and per-gate retrieval evaluators, with HTML, JSON, and CSV reports. Configuration: a central settings module resolving environment variables, then Streamlit secrets, then defaults.', 'Section: THE RAG PIPELINE — RETRIEVAL
A deliberate design touch: the pipeline exposes the fused candidate pool from before re-ranking. This lets the evaluation suite localize exactly where a relevant chunk was lost — at ingestion (it was never indexed), at recall (it never entered the fused pool), or at re-ranking (it entered the pool but was cut from the final top-k) — turning a single aggregate recall number into an actionable, per-stage diagnosis.

The embeddings are asymmetric: documents and queries are embedded with different prefixes ("search_document:" versus "search_query:"), which matches how the embedding models were trained and improves retrieval quality. Because the stored document vectors bake in the document prefix, switching the embedding provider requires rebuilding the vector index.', 'Section: THE RAG PIPELINE — INGESTION
Each section is then split with a recursive character text splitter (about 1000 characters per chunk with 150 characters of overlap, preferring to break on paragraph, line, sentence, then word boundaries). Every chunk is prefixed with its section name for retrieval context ("Section: <name>") and stored with provenance metadata: the source file and a document id. This provenance is essential — it lets any retrieved chunk be traced back to its origin document, which is what makes retrieval evaluation against ground-truth evidence possible.', 'Section: THE RAG PIPELINE — INGESTION
Alongside the per-chunk index, a separate summary index is built. For each document the LLM writes a short factual summary, which is embedded and stored in its own ChromaDB collection. This summary index powers the "broad" retrieval path described below. There is also a lighter in-memory ingestion path for text that is already plain (no file parsing, no summary) — used, for example, when the skill-scorer training corpus is ingested verbatim so the model sees exact text.', 'Section: DEPLOYMENT AND SECURITY
The recruiter chat deploys to a Streamlit-based host such as Streamlit Community Cloud or Hugging Face Spaces, served over HTTPS. Access is gated by a single shared access code, which suits a single-candidate app: there is exactly one person to protect and one link to hand out, so a single secret is the right strength. With no access code configured the gate is a no-op, which is convenient for local development. Once a recruiter authenticates, the result is cached in session state so they are not re-prompted on every interaction.', "Section: THE RAG PIPELINE — RETRIEVAL
Third, hybrid fusion retrieval. For every query variation the system runs both dense vector search (semantic similarity over embeddings in ChromaDB) and sparse keyword search (BM25 Okapi) over the chunk corpus. All the resulting ranked lists are merged with Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF). RRF scores each list independently by reciprocal rank, so a top result in any single list is rewarded regardless of that list's length or origin; this fuses keyword and semantic signals without having to calibrate their raw scores against each other.

Fourth, re-ranking. A cross-encoder reranker rescores the fused candidate pool against the original query and keeps the best top-k chunks, which become the context handed to the agent."]
response:
The **per-gate retrieval analysis** is a diagnostic component of the evaluation suite designed to identify exactly *where* a relevant document chunk was lost in the retrieval pipeline. It operates by analyzing the fused candidate pool (before re-ranking) to determine if a chunk was missed during:

1. **Ingestion** (never indexed)  
2. **Recall** (never entered the fused pool)  
3. **Re-ranking** (entered the pool but excluded from the final top-k)  

This granular localization transforms aggregate recall metrics into actionable insights, enabling precise troubleshooting. The analysis leverages the pipeline's exposure of the fused pool and combines it with ground-truth evidence to pinpoint failures at each stage.  

Key technologies include RAGAS, DeepEval, and custom evaluators, with results visualized in HTML/CSV reports. The process supports checkpointing, resuming, and runs on local models or cloud infrastructure.
reference: For specific document questions it localizes where a relevant chunk was lost in retrieval — at ingestion, at recall (never entered the fused pool), or at re-ranking (cut from the final top-k) — instead of reporting only an aggregate recall number.

user_input: What hybrid search methods are combined in retrieval?
retrieved_contexts:
["Section: THE RAG PIPELINE — RETRIEVAL
Third, hybrid fusion retrieval. For every query variation the system runs both dense vector search (semantic similarity over embeddings in ChromaDB) and sparse keyword search (BM25 Okapi) over the chunk corpus. All the resulting ranked lists are merged with Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF). RRF scores each list independently by reciprocal rank, so a top result in any single list is rewarded regardless of that list's length or origin; this fuses keyword and semantic signals without having to calibrate their raw scores against each other.

Fourth, re-ranking. A cross-encoder reranker rescores the fused candidate pool against the original query and keeps the best top-k chunks, which become the context handed to the agent.", 'Section: DESIGN PRINCIPLES AND WHAT MAKES IT NOTABLE
The standout engineering choices are: a trained, evidence-grounded skill scorer instead of keyword guessing; an agent that routes between verified facts, the scorer, candidate document search, and project search with intelligent fallbacks; a hybrid, fused, re-ranked retrieval pipeline fronted by a broad/specific router and query expansion; a serious multi-dimensional evaluation suite, including the per-gate retrieval failure localization that pinpoints whether recall or re-ranking lost a chunk; a fully synthetic, validated training-data pipeline built because the needed labels did not exist; and a clean build-versus-serve split with three pluggable provider layers that lets one codebase run entirely on free local open-source models or deploy as a cheap, secured, always-on cloud service', 'Section: THE RAG PIPELINE — RETRIEVAL
Retrieval is a multi-stage pipeline designed for both recall and precision.

First, query routing. An LLM router classifies each incoming query as BROAD (a general "who is this person / give me an overview" question) or SPECIFIC (any particular fact, skill, project, or detail). Broad queries are answered from the compact summary index, which gives a clean high-level picture; specific queries go through the full fusion path below.

Second, query expansion. For specific queries the LLM generates several query variations — a deliberate mix of short keyword-style queries, which favor sparse search, and natural-language queries, which favor dense vector search — to maximize recall across both retrieval methods.', 'Section: THE RAG PIPELINE — RETRIEVAL
A deliberate design touch: the pipeline exposes the fused candidate pool from before re-ranking. This lets the evaluation suite localize exactly where a relevant chunk was lost — at ingestion (it was never indexed), at recall (it never entered the fused pool), or at re-ranking (it entered the pool but was cut from the final top-k) — turning a single aggregate recall number into an actionable, per-stage diagnosis.

The embeddings are asymmetric: documents and queries are embedded with different prefixes ("search_document:" versus "search_query:"), which matches how the embedding models were trained and improves retrieval quality. Because the stored document vectors bake in the document prefix, switching the embedding provider requires rebuilding the vector index.', 'Section: TECHNOLOGY STACK
Frontend and app framework: Streamlit, multi-page. LLM and orchestration: Anthropic Claude API (or local Ollama), ReAct-style tool calling, prompt caching, a provider-neutral client. Embeddings: Voyage AI (or local nomic-embed via sentence-transformers). Retrieval: ChromaDB for dense vectors, BM25 Okapi for sparse search, Reciprocal Rank Fusion, and a cross-encoder reranker (Voyage rerank or Qwen3-Reranker). Skill scoring: DeBERTa-v3 with a CORAL ordinal head, in PyTorch and Hugging Face Transformers. Ingestion: Unstructured, PyMuPDF, python-docx, Tesseract OCR, and section-aware recursive chunking via LangChain text splitters. Configuration: a central settings module resolving environment variables, then Streamlit secrets, then defaults.', "Section: THE EVALUATION SUITE
Router Accuracy: did the broad/specific router classify each query correctly against its labeled route?

Per-gate Retrieval Analysis: for specific document questions with known evidence, it localizes exactly where a relevant chunk was lost — at ingestion, at recall, or at re-ranking — using the pre-rerank fused pool the pipeline exposes.

Ingestion Quality: chunk statistics, coverage, and summary quality.

The harness seeds each candidate's data into a fresh collection, runs the pipeline over that candidate's questions, then scores every component on the combined result set and generates an HTML report (with companion JSON and CSV) showing both per-candidate and aggregate breakdowns. It supports checkpointing and resuming, so a long run can be interrupted and continued. Because the LLM judges can themselves run on a free local model, a full evaluation can cost nothing to run.", 'Section: THE RAG PIPELINE — INGESTION
